module-0-student-enrollment-roster.label: Module 0 Project: Student Enrollment Activity Roster
module-0-student-enrollment-roster.summary: Build a qualified and readable student-enrollment roster through five cumulative join steps.

sketch0.title: A Roster That Can Be Trusted

step2.title: Step 2: Give Duplicate IDs Clear Output Headings

step3.title: Step 3: Shorten the Query with Table Aliases

step1.title: Connect Students to Enrollment Records
step1.prompt: Begin the Module 0 Student Enrollment Roster. Return `students.id, students.name, enrollments.id, enrollments.course_id, enrollments.term` from `students` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `students.id = enrollments.student_id`.
step1.hint: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Return `students.id, students.name, enrollments.id, enrollments.course_id, enrollments.term` from `students` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `students.id = enrollments.student_id`.

step1.solutionCode:
SELECT 
  students.id, 
  students.name, 
  enrollments.id, 
  enrollments.course_id, 
  enrollments.term 
FROM students 
INNER JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;

step4.title: Focus on Upper Grade Levels
step4.prompt: Extend the Module 0 Student Enrollment Roster with the next approved requirement. Return `s.id AS student_id, s.name, e.id AS enrollment_id, e.course_id AS course_id, e.term` from `students` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `s.id = e.student_id`. Keep only rows where `s.grade_level >= 10`.

step4.starterCode:
SELECT 
  s.id AS student_id, 
  s.name, 
  e.id AS enrollment_id, 
  e.course_id AS course_id,

FROM students AS s 
INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id;

-- Next, Keep the complete joined roster and include only students in grade level 10 or higher
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step4.solutionCode:
SELECT 
  s.id AS student_id, 
  s.name, 
  e.id AS enrollment_id, 
  e.course_id AS course_id, 
  e.term 
FROM students AS s 
INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id 
WHERE s.grade_level >= 10;

step5.title: Order the Roster Predictably
step5.prompt: Complete the Module 0 Student Enrollment Roster. Return `s.id AS student_id, s.name, e.id AS enrollment_id, e.course_id AS course_id, e.term` from `students` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `s.id = e.student_id`. Keep only rows where `s.grade_level >= 10`. Sort the final result by `s.name ASC, e.course_id ASC`.
step5.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

step5.starterCode:
SELECT 
  s.id AS student_id, 
  s.name, 
  e.id AS enrollment_id, 
  e.course_id AS course_id, 
  e.term 
FROM students AS s 
INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id 
WHERE s.grade_level >= 10;
-- Project step 5: Order the Roster Predictably
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Sort by student name ascending, then course_id ascending, while preserving the complete query
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.

SELECT 
  s.id AS student_id, 
  s.name, 
  e.id AS enrollment_id, 
  e.course_id AS course_id, 
  e.term 
FROM students AS s 
INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id 
WHERE s.grade_level >= 10 
ORDER BY s.name ASC, e.course_id ASC;

-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.

SELECT 
  s.id AS student_id, 
  s.name, 
  e.id AS enrollment_id, 
  e.course_id AS course_id, 
  e.term 
FROM students AS s 
INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id 
WHERE s.grade_level >= 10 
ORDER BY s.name ASC, e.course_id ASC;

sketch0.title: Project brief: student enrollment activity roster
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
The registrar needs a roster that treats each enrollment as a real, traceable record rather than a loose pairing of names and numbers. Your report will begin with the `students` and `enrollments` tables and keep **one row per enrollment**. That grain allows a student to appear more than once when the student has registered for several courses.

The finished roster will identify the enrollment and student with distinct output headings, show the enrollment term and course id, focus on the requested grade levels, and sort the rows predictably. Every selected column should be qualified so its source remains clear. Table aliases will shorten the final query without hiding the relationship.

Build the report in five cumulative steps. Each step starts from the working query completed in the previous step, so preserve earlier columns and conditions as you add the next requirement. By the end, another reader should be able to see what one row represents, where every value came from, and why each enrollment belongs to the student beside it.
